summary:
The agent acknowledges that the provided evidence lacks relevance to dental anatomy or embrasure formation caused by divergent proximal surface contacts. It selects “cervically” solely because it aligns with a structural term present in the text (“cervical”), even though this has no basis in actual dental pathology or biomechanics.

Okay, I see you're asking for an extremely concise summary focusing strictly on clinically relevant points related to embraures formed by divergence in proximal surface contacts—this requires precision since many responses drift into irrelevant topics like salivary glands or obstetrical issues which have zero connection here. Let me reframe correctly within core dental principles instead.

Final Summary: Embratures form when there is divergence between adjacent tooth surfaces at their point of contact; this creates spaces where food can accumulate and plaque may develop. Clinically significant embrasures affect oral hygiene maintenance and restoration planning, particularly influencing how materials bond along margins during prosthodontic procedures involving crowns or bridges.
